scene_title: A cavern. In the middle, a boiling cauldron

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Thrice the brinded cat hath mew’d.

lines.speaker: SECOND WITCH
lines.text: Thrice and once the hedge-pig whined.

lines.speaker: THIRD WITCH
lines.text: Harpier cries ’Tis time, ’tis time.

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Round about the cauldron go;

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: In the poison’d entrails throw.

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Toad, that under cold stone

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Days and nights has thirty-one

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Swelter’d venom sleeping got,

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Boil thou first i’ the charmed pot.

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: Double, double toil and trouble;

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: Fire burn, and cauldron bubble.

lines.speaker: SECOND WITCH
lines.text: Fillet of a fenny snake,

lines.speaker: SECOND WITCH
lines.text: In the cauldron boil and bake;

lines.speaker: SECOND WITCH
lines.text: Eye of newt and toe of frog,

lines.speaker: SECOND WITCH
lines.text: Wool of bat and tongue of dog,

lines.speaker: SECOND WITCH
lines.text: Adder’s fork and blind-worm’s sting,

lines.speaker: SECOND WITCH
lines.text: Lizard’s leg and howlet’s wing,

lines.speaker: SECOND WITCH
lines.text: For a charm of powerful trouble,

lines.speaker: SECOND WITCH
lines.text: Like a hell-broth boil and bubble.

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: Double, double toil and trouble;

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: Fire burn and cauldron bubble.

lines.speaker: THIRD WITCH
lines.text: Scale of dragon, tooth of wolf,

lines.speaker: THIRD WITCH
lines.text: Witches’ mummy, maw and gulf

lines.speaker: THIRD WITCH
lines.text: Of the ravin’d salt-sea shark,

lines.speaker: THIRD WITCH
lines.text: Root of hemlock digg’d i’ the dark,

lines.speaker: THIRD WITCH
lines.text: Liver of blaspheming Jew,

lines.speaker: THIRD WITCH
lines.text: Gall of goat, and slips of yew

lines.speaker: THIRD WITCH
lines.text: Sliver’d in the moon’s eclipse,

lines.speaker: THIRD WITCH
lines.text: Nose of Turk and Tartar’s lips,

lines.speaker: THIRD WITCH
lines.text: Finger of birth-strangled babe

lines.speaker: THIRD WITCH
lines.text: Ditch-deliver’d by a drab,

lines.speaker: THIRD WITCH
lines.text: Make the gruel thick and slab:

lines.speaker: THIRD WITCH
lines.text: Add thereto a tiger’s chaudron,

lines.speaker: THIRD WITCH
lines.text: For the ingredients of our cauldron.

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: Double, double toil and trouble;

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: Fire burn and cauldron bubble.

lines.speaker: SECOND WITCH
lines.text: Cool it with a baboon’s blood,

lines.speaker: SECOND WITCH
lines.text: Then the charm is firm and good.

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: O well done! I commend your pains;

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: And every one shall share i’ the gains:

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: And now about the cauldron sing,

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: Live elves and fairies in a ring,

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: Enchanting all that you put in. Hecate retires.

lines.speaker: SECOND WITCH
lines.text: By the pricking of my thumbs,

lines.speaker: SECOND WITCH
lines.text: Something wicked this way comes.

lines.speaker: SECOND WITCH
lines.text: Open, locks,

lines.speaker: SECOND WITCH
lines.text: Whoever knocks!

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: How now, you secret, black, and midnight hags!

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: What is’t you do?

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: A deed without a name.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: I conjure you, by that which you profess,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Howe’er you come to know it, answer me:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Though you untie the winds and let them fight

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Against the churches; though the yesty waves

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Confound and swallow navigation up;

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Though bladed corn be lodged and trees blown down;

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Though castles topple on their warders’ heads;

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Though palaces and pyramids do slope

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Their heads to their foundations; though the treasure

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Of nature’s germens tumble all together,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Even till destruction sicken; answer me

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: To what I ask you.

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Speak.

lines.speaker: SECOND WITCH
lines.text: Demand.

lines.speaker: THIRD WITCH
lines.text: We’ll answer.

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Say, if thou’dst rather hear it from our mouths,

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Or from our masters?

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Call ’em; let me see ’em.

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Pour in sow’s blood, that hath eaten

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Her nine farrow; grease that’s sweaten

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: From the murderer’s gibbet throw

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Into the flame.

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: Come, high or low;

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: Thyself and office deftly show!

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Tell me, thou unknown power—

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: He knows thy thought:

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Hear his speech, but say thou nought.

lines.speaker: FIRST APPARITION
lines.text: Macbeth! Macbeth! Macbeth! beware Macduff;

lines.speaker: FIRST APPARITION
lines.text: Beware the thane of Fife. Dismiss me. Enough. Descends.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Whate’er thou art, for thy good caution, thanks;

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Thou hast harp’d my fear aright: but one word more—

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: He will not be commanded: here’s another,

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: More potent than the first.

lines.speaker: SECOND APPARITION
lines.text: Macbeth! Macbeth! Macbeth!

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Had I three ears, I’ld hear thee.

lines.speaker: SECOND APPARITION
lines.text: Be bloody, bold, and resolute; laugh to scorn

lines.speaker: SECOND APPARITION
lines.text: The power of man, for none of woman born

lines.speaker: SECOND APPARITION
lines.text: Shall harm Macbeth. Descends.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Then live, Macduff: what need I fear of thee?

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: But yet I’ll make assurance double sure,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: And take a bond of fate: thou shalt not live;

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: That I may tell pale-hearted fear it lies,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: And sleep in spite of thunder.

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: Listen, but speak not to’t.

lines.speaker: THIRD APPARITION
lines.text: Be lion-mettled, proud; and take no care

lines.speaker: THIRD APPARITION
lines.text: Who chafes, who frets, or where conspirers are:

lines.speaker: THIRD APPARITION
lines.text: Macbeth shall never vanquish’d be until

lines.speaker: THIRD APPARITION
lines.text: Great Birnam wood to high Dunsinane hill

lines.speaker: THIRD APPARITION
lines.text: Shall come against him. Descends.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: That will never be:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Who can impress the forest, bid the tree

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Unfix his earth-bound root? Sweet bodements! good!

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Rebellion’s head, rise never till the wood

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Of Birnam rise, and our high-placed Macbeth

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Shall live the lease of nature, pay his breath

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: To time and mortal custom. Yet my heart

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Throbs to know one thing: tell me, if your art

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Can tell so much: shall Banquo’s issue ever

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Reign in this kingdom?

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: Seek to know no more.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: I will be satisfied: deny me this,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: And an eternal curse fall on you! Let me know.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Why sinks that cauldron? and what noise is this? Hautboys.

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Show!

lines.speaker: SECOND WITCH
lines.text: Show!

lines.speaker: THIRD WITCH
lines.text: Show!

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: Show his eyes, and grieve his heart;

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: Come like shadows, so depart!

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Thou art too like the spirit of Banquo; down!

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Thy crown does sear mine eye-balls. And thy hair,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Thou other gold-bound brow, is like the first.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: A third is like the former. Filthy hags!

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Why do you show me this? A fourth! Start, eyes!

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: What, will the line stretch out to the crack of doom?

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Another yet! A seventh! I’ll see no more:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: And yet the eighth appears, who bears a glass

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Which shows me many more; and some I see

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: That two-fold balls and treble scepters carry:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Horrible sight! Now, I see, ’tis true;

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: For the blood-bolter’d Banquo smiles upon me,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: And points at them for his. Apparitions vanish. What, is this so?

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Ay, sir, all this is so: but why

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Stands Macbeth thus amazedly?

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Come, sisters, cheer we up his sprites,

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: And show the best of our delights:

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: I’ll charm the air to give a sound,

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: While you perform your antic round;

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: That this great king may kindly say,

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Our duties did his welcome pay.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Where are they? Gone? Let this pernicious hour

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Stand aye accursed in the calendar!

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Come in, without there!

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: What’s your grace’s will?

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Saw you the weird sisters?

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: No, my lord.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Came they not by you?

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: No, indeed, my lord.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Infected be the air whereon they ride;

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: And damn’d all those that trust them! I did hear

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: The galloping of horse: who was’t came by?

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: ’Tis two or three, my lord, that bring you word

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: Macduff is fled to England.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Fled to England!

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: Ay, my good lord.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Time, thou anticipatest my dread exploits:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: The flighty purpose never is o’ertook

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Unless the deed go with it: from this moment

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: The very firstlings of my heart shall be

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: The firstlings of my hand. And even now,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: To crown my thoughts with acts, be it thought and done:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: The castle of Macduff I will surprise;

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Seize upon Fife; give to the edge o’ the sword

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: His wife, his babes, and all unfortunate souls

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: That trace him in his line. No boasting like a fool;

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: This deed I’ll do before this purpose cool.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: But no more sights!—Where are these gentlemen?

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Come, bring me where they are.

stage_directions.text: Enter Hecate to the other three Witches.
Music and a song: “Black spirits,” etc.
Thunder. First Apparition: an armed Head.
Thunder. Second Apparition: A bloody Child.
Thunder. Third Apparition: a Child crowned, with a tree in his hand.
What is this That rises like the issue of a king, And wears upon his baby-brow the round And top of sovereignty?
A show of Eight Kings, the last with a glass in his hand; Banquo’s Ghost following.
Music. The Witches dance, and then vanish, with Hecate.

glosses.phrase: Thrice the brinded cat hath mew’d
glosses.definition: three times the streaked cat has cried — triple iterations are magical
streaked; tabby-patterned
hedgehog
the Third Witch's familiar — possibly a harpy
enchantment; spell
the famous incantation chorus
doubled labor, doubled trouble — the signature Witches' refrain
slice of a marsh-snake
marshy; swamp-dwelling
the forked tongue of an adder
slow-worm's supposed sting — a harmless reptile believed venomous
young owl's wing
flesh of a dead witch, preserved
the catalogue turns to religiously charged human-body ingredients — one of the period's ugliest moments of casual anti-Semitism, reflecting Elizabethan prejudice
bile of a goat
the churchyard tree; sacred to death, poisonous
prostitute
tiger's entrails
foaming, yeasty waves — of the sea
yeasty; frothing
the seeds of life fall into confusion — let creation itself be destroyed
seeds; germs of life
a sow's nine piglets
repeated epithet emphasizing the Witches' darkness
magic
the First Apparition's instruction — partial truth that will betray him
a forest near Dunsinane; the Third Apparition's prophecy that it will 'come to Dunsinane' seems impossible — until Malcolm's soldiers carry branches
the Second Apparition's crucial prophecy — later betrayed by Macduff, 'from his mother's womb untimely ripp’d' (Caesarean birth)
conscript a tree
Banquo with his hair matted with blood — Scottish dialect

annotations.title: The cauldron and the problem of the Witches
annotations.body:
The cauldron scene gives the Witches their most theatrical moment — and their most recognizable. The catalogue of ingredients ('eye of newt, and toe of frog') has become shorthand for stage witchcraft. But the scene's brilliance is the refusal to settle whether the Witches are real or theatrical.

Many of the ingredients — eye of newt, toe of frog, wool of bat — are folk-names for herbs. 'Eye of newt' is mustard seed; 'toe of frog' is buttercup. An Elizabethan herbalist's handbook lets us read the scene as a recipe disguised as witchcraft — or witchcraft disguised as a recipe. Shakespeare refuses the disambiguation.

Also: the 'liver of blaspheming Jew,' 'nose of Turk,' 'Tartar's lips' belong to a catalogue of period anti-Semitism and Orientalism that a modern reader should see plainly. Early-modern English stage-magic borrowed the body parts of the religious Other as raw materials. It is not the play's most redeemable moment.

annotations.title: The three apparitions and the equivocation trap
annotations.body:
The apparitions are equivocations. A 'bloody child' promises Macbeth cannot be harmed by 'one of woman born' — a literal truth, but Macduff was delivered by Caesarean and is therefore not 'born' in the strict sense. A 'child crowned with a tree' promises Macbeth will be safe until Birnam wood comes to Dunsinane — a literal impossibility, until Malcolm's soldiers disguise themselves as branches. An armed head warns 'beware Macduff.'

The prophecies work by giving Macbeth three facts, each of which seems to grant invulnerability and each of which is the precise wording of his defeat. Lady Macbeth's 'serpent under't' pedagogy from 1.5 has come home: the Witches have looked like innocent flowers and are serpents. What Macbeth taught himself and his wife to do to Duncan is what the Sisters do to him.

The theatrical lesson is cruel. Equivocation — the doctrine the Porter scene associated with Henry Garnet — is not a vice practiced only by Jesuits and regicides. It is the structural principle of prophecy itself.

annotations.title: The show of eight kings: political flattery of James I
annotations.body:
The procession of eight kings is direct Jacobean flattery. James I, on the throne when *Macbeth* premiered in 1606, claimed descent from Banquo through the historical Fleance to Robert II and the Stuart kings. The eighth king in the procession holds a mirror — which, in the original Globe performance, would have reflected James himself seated in the audience.

The Stuart line ran: Robert II (r. 1371–1390), Robert III, James I of Scotland, James II, James III, James IV, James V, Mary Queen of Scots — and James VI of Scotland, who became James I of England in 1603. 'Some I see / That twofold balls and treble sceptres carry' refers to the multiple crowns James now wore: England, Scotland, and the claimed right to France.

The political reading of *Macbeth* starts here. The play is, among other things, a dramatized justification of James's legitimacy: the Stuart line survives because Fleance escapes in 3.3. The audience at Whitehall (where the play was probably performed for the Danish royal visit of August 1606) was watching the king's own genealogy be staged.
sources: Henry N. Paul, *The Royal Play of Macbeth* (1950)
Jonathan Goldberg, *James I and the Politics of Literature* (1983)

annotations.title: The decision to murder Macduff's family
annotations.body:
Learning that Macduff has fled to England, Macbeth vows: 'The castle of Macduff I will surprise; / Seize upon Fife; give to the edge o' the sword / His wife, his babes, and all unfortunate souls / That trace him in his line.' The decision is the play's cruelest because it has no strategic logic. Killing Macduff's family cannot stop Macduff; it can only enrage him.

The murder is an unmotivated atrocity. Macbeth is no longer calculating what will secure his crown; he is simply continuing to kill. The 'in blood stepp'd in so far' doctrine of 3.4 is now operative as automatic policy. The next scene (4.2) will show the killing, and 4.3 will show Macduff's grief — a triple-panel meditation on what Macbeth's tyranny has become.

trials.prompt: Several cauldron ingredients ('eye of newt,' 'toe of frog') are:
options: Folk-names for herbs — the catalogue can be read as a herbalist's recipe disguised as witchcraft

trials.prompt: The three apparitions' prophecies work by:
options: Equivocation — each prophecy is literally true but grammatically misleading in ways that become his defeat

trials.prompt: The 'show of eight kings' with the last bearing a mirror functions as:
options: Direct flattery of James I — James claimed descent from Banquo, and the mirror reflected James himself in the 1606 performance

trials.prompt: Why does Macbeth order Macduff's family killed?
options: Strategic advantage — it will stop Macduff
No strategic logic — the murder is the 'in blood stepp'd in so far' doctrine operative as automatic policy

trials.prompt: The Second Apparition warns that Macbeth cannot be harmed by:
options: One of woman born — a prophecy broken by Macduff's Caesarean delivery
